summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

An electron moves at 2 x 10^6 m/s perpendicular to a 0.5 T field. What is the force?

F = qvB = (1.6e-19)(2e6)(0.5) = 1.6e-13 N.

What is the 'Lorentz force law'?

The Lorentz force law gives the total EM force on a charge: F = qE + qv x B.

What is the 'Hall effect'?

The Hall effect: B deflects current carriers sideways, creating a measurable transverse voltage.

What is the 'electromagnetic force' as a fundamental force?

The electromagnetic force governs all electric and magnetic phenomena and is mediated by photons.

What is 'gyration' of a charged particle?

Gyration is the circular component of motion perpendicular to B. The gyration frequency is f = qB/(2*pi*m).

What is the 'Hall voltage'?

The Hall voltage develops when the electric force from charge buildup balances the magnetic deflection force.

What is Thomson's experiment?

J.J. Thomson measured e/m for electrons using crossed fields, discovering the electron as a subatomic particle.

In a mass spectrometer, C-12 and C-13 ions enter a 0.5 T field at the same speed. If C-12 has radius 12 cm, what is C-13's radius?

r is proportional to m: r_13 = 12 * (13/12) = 13 cm.

A charged particle passes through a velocity selector (E/B = 10^5 m/s), then curves in B = 0.2 T with r = 0.1 m. If q = +e, what is its mass?

m = qBr/v = (1.6e-19)(0.2)(0.1)/1e5 = 3.2e-26 kg.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the 'Hall voltage'?

The Hall voltage develops when the electric force from charge buildup balances the magnetic deflection force.

Example: An electromagnetic flowmeter uses B = 0.1 T across a 5 mm artery. Hall voltage = 0.5 mV. Blood flow speed is:

v = V_H/(Bd) = 5e-4/(0.1*5e-3) = 5e-4/5e-4 = 1.0 m/s.
